qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.title: Nested repeat extglob patterns cause ReDoS via catastrophic regex backtracking
qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.advisory_match_reason: Advisory describes ReDoS from repeated '*' wildcards (e.g., '***************X***') that compile to multiple [^/]*? lazy quantifiers causing exponential backtracking across all possible splits. Finding describes ReDoS from nested extglob patterns (e.g., '+(+(a))') that compile to nested greedy quantifiers (e.g., /^(?:(?:a)+)+$/). Different pattern syntax, different regex structure, different code path in minimatch. While both are ReDoS vulnerabilities in the same package, they represent distinct root causes and vulnerable code locations.

target_advisory_judgement.reason: The qualifying finding does not match the advisory-described vulnerable code or root cause. Advisory describes ReDoS from repeated '*' wildcards that compile to multiple [^/]*? lazy quantifiers, while the finding describes ReDoS from nested extglob quantifiers. The advisory root cause is specifically about how '*' wildcards are compiled and the exponential backtracking across their splits, not about extglob nesting or greedy quantifier nesting.
evidence: Advisory: ReDoS from repeated '*' wildcards (e.g., '***************X***') compiling to multiple [^/]*? groups with O(4^N) backtracking
Finding: ReDoS from nested extglob patterns (e.g., '+(+(a))') compiling to nested greedy quantifiers with O(2^N) backtracking
Different vulnerable code paths: advisory about '*' wildcard compilation, finding about extglob-to-regex compilation in src/ast.ts
Same package (minimatch) and same vulnerability class (ReDoS) but different root causes and vulnerable code locations
